content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Derek McDonald 
Attorney 
East Bay Municipal Utility District 
375 Eleventh Street 
Oakland, CA 94607-4240 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-156 
Dear Mr. McDonald: 
September 12, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the financial disclosure provisions 
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accu~ate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
(1) When does a "Standby Officer," as defined by the California Emergency Services Act2, 
assume office for purposes of the Act's financial disclosure requirements? 
(2) Can an official assume office for purposes of the Act prior to being sworn in? 
CONCLUSIONS 
( 1) A "Standby Officer" assumes office on the earlier of either the date that the Standby 
Officer is authorized to serve or the date that the Standby Officer begins to perform the 
duties of the assumed position. Based on the facts provided, appointment to the Board as 
a Standby Officer would constitute a circumstance in which an officer is "authorized to 
serve," and thus trigger the Act's requirement that the officer file an "Assuming Office 
Statement of Economic Interests" within 30 days of the date of appointment. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 !000 through 9IO 14. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 The California Emergency Services Act is contained in Sections 8550 through 8668. 


File No. A-18-156 
Page No. 2 
(2) Yes. The date an official assumes office for purposes of the Act can occur before the 
official is sworn in. 
BACKGROUND AND ANALYSIS 
You are an attorney with the East Bay Municipal Utility District (the "EBMUD") authorized 
to request advice on behalf of the EB MUD Board of Directors. The Board, which serves as the 
EBMUD's governing body, consists of seven elected members from seven district wards. 
Per the California Emergency Services Act ("CESA"), a governing body may appoint up to 
three "Standby Officers" for each seat on the body to serve if a sitting member becomes unavailable 
during an emergency. An officer is "unavailable" for purposes of the CESA, ifhe or she is "killed, 
missing, or so seriously injured as to be unable to attend meetings and otherwise perform his 
duties." (Section 8636.) The authority to find a member "unavailable" lies with the governing body. 
(Ibid.) 
Upon determination that a sitting member is unavailable, the Standby Officer may serve on 
the governing body until he or she is dismissed, the sitting member is no longer unavailable, or the 
next election for the seat. (Sections 8640 and 8641 ( d).) 
Prior to assuming the seat of an unavailable member, the duties of a Standby Officer are 
limited to the following: 
• 
To inform himself or herself of the duties of the office for which the officer stands 
by. 
• 
To keep informed of the business and affairs of the political subdivision to the 
extent necessary to enable the standby officer to fill his or her post competently. 
• 
To immediately report himself or herself ready for duty in the event of a state of 
war emergency or in the event of a state of emergency or a local emergency at the 
place and in the method previously designated by the political subdivision. 
• 
To fill the post for which he or she has been appointed when the regular officer is 
unavailable during a state of war emergency, a state of emergency or a local 
emergency. 
(Section 8641(a) through (d).) 
The conflict of interest code for the EB MUD classifies Board Members as "officials who 
manage public investments" who must "file a statement of economic interests pursuant to 
Government Code Section 87200." 


Financial Disclosure Requirements 
File No. A-18-156 
Page No. 3 
The Act requires an individual holding a public office enumerated in Section 87200, 
including an office charged with the management of public investments, to file a Statement of 
Economic Interests ("Form 700") disclosing his or her financial interests. (Section 87200 et seq.) 
Subdivision ( c) of Regulation 18722 extends this disclosure requirement to an individual serving as 
an alternate or designee for an office enumerated in Section 87200 and provides in pertinent part as 
follows: 
"Every person who holds an office specified in Government Code sections 
87200 ... as an alternate or as a designee, shall be required to disclose his or her 
economic interests in the same manner as any other person holding the office." 
In this case, Standby Officers are alternates or designees for purposes of Regulation 18722. 
Additionally, the EBMUD's conflict of interest code provides that Board Members are "officials 
who manage public investments" for purposes of Section 87200. Therefore, Board Members are 
subject to the Act's financial disclosure requirements and must file Form 700s. As alternates or 
designees of the Board Members, Standby Officers must also file Form 700s. 
Assuming Office Date 
The Act requires that a person holding an office specified in Section 87200 must file a Form 
700 with their filing officer within 30 days of assuming office. 
The date a person assumes office for purposes of this filing requirement is set forth in 
Regulation 18722( a)(l ), which states that: 
"( 1) The date of assuming office is the date that a person either: 
"(A) Is authorized to serve in the position, as by being sworn in; or 
"(B) Except in the case of an elected official who is required to be sworn into office, 
begins to perform the duties of the position, as by making, participating in making, 
or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision; 
whichever occurs earlier." 
Being appointed as a Standby Officer by the Board would constitute an ·authorization to 
serve in the position, and thus trigger the filing of a Form 700 within 30 days of the date of 
appointment. 
Assuming Office and Being Sworn In 
We note that you also inquired as to whether an official could "assume office" for purposes 
of the Act before being sworn in. Regulation 18722 indicates that "being sworn in" is merely an 
example of being "authorized to serve," not a required element of assuming office. Therefore, we 
find that an official could "assume office" for purposes of the Act prior to being sworn in. 


File No. A-18-156 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
AES:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
Adam E. Silver 
By: 
Counsel, Legal Division

1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 !000 through 9IO 14. 2 The California Emergency Services Act is contained in Sections 8550 through 8668.  
(2) Yes. The date an official assumes office for purposes of the Act can occur before the 
official is sworn in.

We note that you also inquired as to whether an official could "assume office" for purposes
